question: Which IEEE standard defines Ethernet (802.3) frame format and CSMA/CD access control?
explanation: IEEE 802.3 defines Ethernet standards including frame format and CSMA/CD. 802.11 is Wi-Fi, 802.5 is Token Ring, 802.1Q is VLAN tagging.

question: What is the maximum cable length for a 1000BASE-T (Gigabit Ethernet) segment?
explanation: 1000BASE-T over Cat5e/Cat6 UTP has a maximum segment length of 100 meters, same as 10BASE-T and 100BASE-TX.

question: Which protocol prevents broadcast storms in a network with redundant links?
explanation: STP (IEEE 802.1D) prevents broadcast storms by placing redundant ports in a blocking state, ensuring a loop-free topology.

question: What is the primary purpose of VLAN (Virtual LAN)?

options.text: To logically segment a physical network into separate broadcast domains
To increase physical port density on a switch
To load balance traffic across multiple links

explanation: VLANs create logical broadcast domains on a single physical switch, reducing broadcast traffic and improving security.

question: Which Ethernet standard uses fiber optic cabling and supports 10 Gbps at up to 40 km?
explanation: 10GBASE-ER (Extended Range) uses single-mode fiber and supports 10 Gbps up to 40 km. 10GBASE-T uses copper, CX4 uses twinax.

question: In a collapsed core network architecture, which layers are combined into a single device?

options.text: All three layers into one device

explanation: A collapsed core architecture combines the core and distribution layers into a single device, suitable for smaller networks.

question: What is the default VLAN ID assigned to untagged frames on a trunk port?
explanation: VLAN 1 is the default native VLAN. Untagged frames on a trunk port are associated with the native VLAN.

question: Which WAN technology uses cell switching with fixed 53-byte cells?
explanation: ATM uses fixed 53-byte cells (5-byte header + 48-byte payload) for efficient switching. Frame Relay uses variable-length frames.

question: What does Rapid Spanning Tree Protocol (RSTP, 802.1w) improve over traditional STP?

options.text: Faster convergence time from 50 seconds to a few seconds

explanation: RSTP reduces convergence from 50 seconds (STP) to a few seconds by introducing rapid transition to forwarding state and new port roles.

question: Which type of Ethernet framing adds a 4-byte VLAN tag (802.1Q) to the frame?
explanation: 802.1Q inserts a 4-byte tag between the source MAC and EtherType fields, carrying VLAN ID (12 bits) and priority (3 bits).

question: What is the primary advantage of MPLS over traditional IP routing?

options.text: Faster packet forwarding using labels instead of longest-prefix match
Built-in quality of service for voice traffic

explanation: MPLS uses label-switched paths (LSPs) for fast forwarding. Packets are switched based on labels, avoiding full IP routing table lookups.

question: In SD-WAN architecture, what determines the path selection for traffic?

options.text: Application-aware policies considering link quality, cost, and SLA requirements

explanation: SD-WAN uses application-aware policies that consider real-time link quality metrics (latency, jitter, loss), cost, and SLA to select the optimal path.

question: Which protocol is used to dynamically learn MAC addresses on a LAN switch?

options.text: The switch learns MAC addresses from the source address of incoming frames
ARP is used to learn MAC addresses on switches
Switches use ICMP to discover MAC addresses
RARP protocol is used for MAC learning

explanation: LAN switches dynamically build their MAC address table by examining the source MAC address of each incoming frame and associating it with the ingress port.

question: What is the maximum number of VLANs supported by IEEE 802.1Q?
explanation: 802.1Q uses a 12-bit VLAN ID field, supporting 4096 values (0-4095). VLAN 0 and 4095 are reserved, leaving 4094 usable.

question: Which network topology provides the highest redundancy for enterprise campus networks?
explanation: Full mesh provides the highest redundancy with direct links between all nodes. It is expensive but offers no single point of failure.

question: What is the purpose of EtherChannel (IEEE 802.3ad / LACP)?

options.text: To bundle multiple physical links into one logical link for increased bandwidth and redundancy
To create VLAN trunks between switches
To compress Ethernet frames for transmission
To convert between Ethernet and fiber optic media

explanation: EtherChannel/LACP aggregates multiple physical links into one logical link, providing increased bandwidth and automatic failover.

question: Which VLAN type carries traffic for multiple VLANs between switches?
explanation: Trunk ports carry traffic for multiple VLANs using 802.1Q tags. Access ports carry traffic for only one VLAN.

question: What happens when a broadcast storm occurs in a network?

options.text: Excessive broadcast traffic floods the network, causing congestion and degradation
The network automatically segments into smaller subnets
Routers filter out all broadcast traffic
Switches increase their forwarding rates to compensate

explanation: A broadcast storm occurs when broadcast packets multiply due to switching loops, flooding the network and potentially causing complete failure.

question: Which technology allows multiple VLANs to share a single physical interface on a router?
explanation: Router on a Stick uses subinterfaces on a single physical router interface, each with a different VLAN ID (802.1Q tagging) to route between VLANs.

question: What is the purpose of BPDU Guard in STP configuration?

options.text: To prevent unauthorized switches from becoming the root bridge
To load balance across multiple spanning trees

explanation: BPDU Guard disables a port if it receives a BPDU, preventing unauthorized devices from participating in STP.

question: In a three-tier network hierarchy, which layer provides routing between VLANs and policy enforcement?
explanation: The distribution layer provides inter-VLAN routing, ACLs, QoS policies, and route summarization between the access and core layers.

question: Which feature allows a switch to mirror traffic from one port to another for monitoring?
explanation: SPAN copies traffic from source ports/VLANs to a destination port where a monitoring device can analyze it.

question: What is the maximum number of hops in a Spanning Tree domain before a BPDU is discarded?
explanation: STP has a maximum hop count of 20. BPDUs received after traversing 20 hops are discarded to prevent infinite loops.

question: Which WAN technology provides a dedicated, always-on connection at guaranteed bandwidth?
explanation: Leased lines (T1/E1, T3/E3) provide dedicated, point-to-point connections with guaranteed bandwidth. Frame Relay and X.25 are shared.

question: What is the function of a Layer 3 switch compared to a traditional router?

options.text: It performs routing in hardware (ASIC) for faster inter-VLAN routing
It replaces the need for any Layer 2 devices

explanation: Layer 3 switches route packets in hardware using ASICs, providing much faster inter-VLAN routing than software-based routing.

question: Which protocol automates VLAN configuration across multiple switches?
explanation: VTP allows a VTP server to distribute VLAN configuration to all switches in the VTP domain, centralizing VLAN management.

question: What is the difference between half-duplex and full-duplex Ethernet?

options.text: Full-duplex allows simultaneous send/receive; half-duplex does not
Half-duplex allows simultaneous send/receive; full-duplex does not
There is no practical difference in modern networks

explanation: Full-duplex allows simultaneous bidirectional communication. Half-duplex only allows one direction at a time. Modern switched networks use full-duplex.

question: In MSTP (Multiple Spanning Tree Protocol), what can be achieved that RSTP alone cannot?

options.text: Different spanning tree instances for different VLAN groups
Automatic load balancing across all links

explanation: MSTP (802.1s) maps multiple VLANs to a single spanning tree instance, allowing different VLAN groups to use different root bridges.

question: What is the maximum data rate of 1000BASE-LX over single-mode fiber?

options.text: 1 Gbps up to 100 m over copper

explanation: 1000BASE-LX uses long-wavelength laser (1310 nm) on single-mode fiber up to 5 km at 1 Gbps.

question: Which layer of the TCP/IP model corresponds to the OSI Data Link and Physical layers?
explanation: The TCP/IP Network Access layer maps to OSI layers 1 and 2 (Physical and Data Link).

question: What is the default TCP maximum segment size (MSS) when no option is specified?
explanation: TCP default MSS is 536 bytes (576 - 20 IP header - 20 TCP header = 536). The common MSS for Ethernet is 1460 bytes when path MTU is 1500.

question: Which protocol is used to resolve an IP address to a MAC address?
explanation: ARP resolves IP addresses to MAC addresses by broadcasting a request. RARP does the reverse (MAC to IP).

question: What does the TCP three-way handshake consist of?
explanation: TCP connection establishment: client sends SYN, server responds with SYN-ACK, client sends ACK. This is the three-way handshake.

question: Which field in the IPv4 header is decremented by each router to prevent infinite loops?
explanation: TTL is decremented by each router. When it reaches zero, the packet is discarded and an ICMP Time Exceeded message is sent.

question: What is the primary difference between TCP and UDP?

options.text: TCP is connection-oriented with reliability; UDP is connectionless without guaranteed delivery
UDP supports more data per packet than TCP
TCP operates at the network layer; UDP at the transport layer

explanation: TCP provides connection-oriented, reliable delivery with sequencing and flow control. UDP is connectionless, faster, with no delivery guarantee.

question: Which DNS record type maps a domain name to an IPv4 address?
explanation: A record maps a domain name to an IPv4 address. AAAA maps to IPv6. CNAME is an alias. MX is for mail servers.

question: What is the purpose of ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol)?

options.text: To report errors and provide diagnostic information about network conditions
To resolve domain names to IP addresses

explanation: ICMP reports errors (destination unreachable, TTL exceeded) and diagnostics (ping, traceroute). It is a companion protocol to IP.

question: Which HTTP status code indicates a resource has been permanently moved?
explanation: 301 indicates permanent redirection. 302 is temporary. 200 is success. 404 is not found.

question: What is the well-known port number for HTTPS?
explanation: HTTPS uses port 443. HTTP uses port 80. 8080 is an alternative HTTP port. 22 is SSH.

question: Which TCP flag is used to initiate a connection?
explanation: SYN (Synchronize) flag is used to initiate a TCP connection. ACK acknowledges receipt. FIN terminates. RST resets the connection.

question: What protocol is used to send email from a client to a mail server?
explanation: SMTP (port 25/587) sends email from client to server and between servers. POP3/IMAP retrieve email from servers. FTP is file transfer.

question: How many bits are in an IPv4 address?
explanation: IPv4 addresses are 32 bits (4 octets). IPv6 addresses are 128 bits. MAC addresses are 48 bits.

question: What is the TCP sliding window mechanism used for?

options.text: Flow control to prevent the sender from overwhelming the receiver

explanation: The TCP sliding window controls the amount of unacknowledged data in flight, implementing flow control so the sender does not overwhelm the receiver.

question: Which protocol operates at the Application layer and is used for secure file transfer?
explanation: SFTP operates over SSH and provides encrypted file transfer. TFTP is simple/no-security. FTP is unencrypted. SCP is also over SSH.

question: What is the purpose of NAT (Network Address Translation)?

options.text: To translate private IP addresses to public addresses for internet access
To compress IP packets for transmission

explanation: NAT translates private (RFC 1918) addresses to public addresses, allowing multiple internal hosts to share one or few public IPs.

question: Which TCP congestion avoidance algorithm gradually increases the sending rate until packet loss occurs?
explanation: AIMD increases the congestion window linearly (additive) and decreases it multiplicatively on packet loss, balancing throughput and fairness.

question: What does the DNS recursive query do?

options.text: The DNS server resolves the query completely and returns the final answer
The DNS server returns only cached results
The client must resolve the query itself
The DNS server forwards to another server only

explanation: In a recursive query, the DNS server takes full responsibility for resolving the query, iterating through the DNS hierarchy if needed.

question: Which protocol uses port 53?
explanation: DNS uses both UDP and TCP on port 53. DHCP uses 67/68. NTP uses 123. LDAP uses 389.

question: What is the maximum segment size of an Ethernet frame (MTU)?
explanation: Standard Ethernet MTU is 1500 bytes. Jumbo frames can be up to 9000 bytes. IP MTU is typically 1500 bytes.

question: Which HTTP method is used to update an existing resource?
explanation: PUT replaces an existing resource or creates one at a specific URL. POST creates a new resource. GET retrieves. DELETE removes.

question: What is the role of the DHCP ACK message?

options.text: To confirm the IP address lease offered to the client
To request a specific IP address
To release an IP address lease

explanation: DHCP ACK confirms the lease after the client sends a DHCP REQUEST. The DORA process: Discover, Offer, Request, ACK.

question: Which transport layer protocol is used by DNS for most queries?
explanation: DNS primarily uses UDP for queries (port 53). TCP is used for zone transfers and when responses exceed 512 bytes.

question: What is CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) notation for the subnet mask 255.255.255.0?
explanation: 255.255.255.0 has 24 consecutive 1-bits, so CIDR notation is /24. /16 is 255.255.0.0, /8 is 255.0.0.0, /32 is a single host.

question: Which protocol provides secure remote shell access with encryption?
explanation: SSH provides encrypted remote access on port 22. Telnet sends everything in cleartext. Rlogin is also unencrypted.

question: What happens when TCP receives three duplicate ACKs?

options.text: Fast retransmit is triggered, resending the suspected lost segment
A new SYN is sent to restart the connection

explanation: Three duplicate ACKs trigger fast retransmit without waiting for the retransmission timer, reducing latency from lost segments.

question: Which IP address range is defined as Class A private address space (RFC 1918)?
explanation: Class A private: 10.0.0.0/8. Class B: 172.16.0.0/12. Class C: 192.168.0.0/16. 169.254.0.0/16 is APIPA/link-local.

question: What is the purpose of TCP FIN flag?
explanation: FIN indicates the sender has finished sending data and wants to close the connection. The four-way termination uses FIN, ACK, FIN, ACK.

question: What is the difference between ARP and RARP?

options.text: ARP resolves IP to MAC; RARP resolves MAC to IP
ARP resolves MAC to IP; RARP resolves IP to MAC

explanation: ARP maps IP addresses to MAC addresses. RARP (Reverse ARP) maps MAC addresses to IP addresses, now largely replaced by DHCP.

question: Which protocol uses TLS to provide secure communication over a network?
explanation: HTTPS (HTTP over TLS/SSL) encrypts HTTP traffic on port 443. Standard FTP, SMTP, and HTTP are unencrypted.

question: Which routing protocol uses Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm and maintains a link-state database?
explanation: OSPF uses Dijkstra's SPF algorithm with a link-state database. BGP uses path-vector, RIP uses distance-vector (Bellman-Ford).

question: What is the administrative distance of a directly connected route?
explanation: Directly connected routes have AD 0 (most trusted). Static routes have AD 1. EIGRP 90. OSPF 110. RIP 120.

question: Which BGP attribute is used to prefer one exit point over another within an AS?
explanation: LOCAL_PREF is exchanged within an AS and influences outbound path selection. Higher LOCAL_PREF is preferred.

question: What is the maximum hop count for RIP?
explanation: RIP max hop count is 15. A hop count of 16 means unreachable. This limits RIP to small networks.

question: In OSPF, what is the purpose of Area 0?

options.text: It is the backbone area that all other areas must connect to
It is used for external route redistribution
It is the default area for all interfaces

explanation: OSPF Area 0 is the backbone. All non-backbone areas must connect to Area 0 directly or via virtual link.

question: Which protocol natively supports VLSM and CIDR?

options.text: Both OSPF and EIGRP support VLSM

explanation: Link-state protocols (OSPF, IS-IS) and EIGRP, RIPv2, BGP all support VLSM. Only RIPv1 (classful) does not.

question: What is route redistribution?

options.text: Injecting routes learned by one routing protocol into another

explanation: Route redistribution allows different routing protocols to share routes, enabling multi-protocol environments.

question: Which OSPF LSA type describes routes to external networks?
explanation: Type 5 LSAs carry external routes from an ASBR. Type 1 = router, Type 2 = network (DR), Type 3 = inter-area summary.

question: What is the purpose of a default route (0.0.0.0/0)?

options.text: To forward all unmatched traffic to a designated next-hop
To load balance across all interfaces
To define administrative distance for all routes

explanation: A default route matches any destination not found in the routing table, acting as gateway of last resort.

question: How does OSPF elect a Designated Router on a multi-access network?

options.text: Highest router priority, then highest router ID
Highest IP address on the interface

explanation: DR election: highest priority (default 1) wins, ties broken by highest router ID. Priority 0 means ineligible.

question: What is the difference between OSPF and IS-IS?

options.text: OSPF uses IP; IS-IS is CLNS-based and protocol-independent
OSPF is distance-vector; IS-IS is link-state

explanation: Both are link-state. OSPF operates over IP; IS-IS over CLNS. IS-IS scales better and is widely used in ISP backbones.

question: What is a routing loop and how does split horizon help?

options.text: A loop causes packets to circulate endlessly; split horizon prevents advertising routes back out the same interface
Loops are caused by DNS failures
Loops only occur in link-state protocols

explanation: Split horizon prevents advertising routes back out the interface they were learned on, breaking distance-vector loops.

question: Which BGP message type advertises reachable destinations?
explanation: BGP UPDATE carries NLRI and path attributes. OPEN establishes sessions, KEEPALIVE maintains them, NOTIFICATION reports errors.

question: What differentiates interior gateway protocols (IGPs) from exterior gateway protocols (EGPs)?

options.text: IGPs operate within one AS; EGPs route between different ASes
EGPs use link-state; IGPs use distance-vector

explanation: IGPs (OSPF, EIGRP, RIP) work within an AS. EGPs (BGP) exchange routes between autonomous systems.

question: What is an OSPF stub area?

options.text: An area that does not receive external (Type 5) LSAs
An area with only one router
An area that blocks all hello packets

explanation: Stub areas filter Type 5 external LSAs, using a default route (Type 3) instead, reducing LSDB size.

question: What metric does OSPF use for path selection?
explanation: OSPF cost = reference bandwidth / interface bandwidth. Default reference is 100 Mbps. Lower cost is preferred.

question: What is the default administrative distance of OSPF?
explanation: OSPF AD = 110. Connected = 0, Static = 1, EIGRP = 90, RIP = 120, eBGP = 20, iBGP = 200.

question: What does BGP AS_PATH prepend do?

options.text: Lengthens the AS_PATH to make a route less preferred for inbound traffic engineering
Shortens the AS_PATH for faster convergence

explanation: AS_PATH prepend adds extra AS numbers to make a route appear longer and less attractive to inbound traffic.

question: What is Equal-Cost Multi-Path (ECMP) routing?

options.text: Load sharing across multiple paths with equal cost

explanation: ECMP allows routers to use multiple equal-cost paths simultaneously for load sharing.

question: Which routing protocol converges fastest after a topology change?
explanation: EIGRP converges fastest due to DUAL maintaining feasible successors. OSPF is second. RIP is slowest.

question: What is a floating static route?

options.text: A backup route with a higher administrative distance than the primary
A way to convert dynamic to static routes

explanation: Floating static routes have a higher AD than the primary route, activating only when the primary fails.

question: What is longest prefix match in routing?

options.text: When multiple routes match, the most specific (longest prefix) is selected
The first matching route is used
The route with the lowest metric is always chosen

explanation: Longest prefix match is fundamental: the route with the most specific matching prefix wins.

question: Which protocol uses the DUAL (Diffusing Update Algorithm)?
explanation: EIGRP uses DUAL to ensure loop-free paths by maintaining successors and feasible successors.

question: What is the BGP synchronization rule?

options.text: iBGP should not advertise routes until they are also known via IGP (now disabled by default)
BGP routes must match DNS records
All peers must have identical tables
Updates must be acknowledged by all

explanation: BGP sync prevented black-holing by requiring IGP awareness. Disabled by default in modern configurations.

question: What triggers an OSPF SPF recalculation?

options.text: A change in the link-state database

explanation: SPF runs when the LSDB changes. OSPF implements SPF throttling to limit computation frequency.

question: Which BGP session type is between routers in the same AS?
explanation: iBGP runs within one AS. eBGP connects different ASes.

question: What is route summarization (aggregation)?

options.text: Combining multiple specific routes into a single broader prefix

explanation: Summarization reduces routing table size by advertising a broader prefix encompassing specific routes.

question: Which protocol is classified as a hybrid routing protocol?
explanation: EIGRP is hybrid: distance-vector properties with link-state features like fast convergence.

question: What is an OSPF virtual link?

options.text: A logical connection to Area 0 through a transit area
A way to increase hello interval

explanation: Virtual links provide connectivity to Area 0 when a non-backbone area cannot connect directly.

question: What is BGP MED (Multi-Exit Discriminator)?

options.text: A non-transitive attribute suggesting a preferred entry point to a neighboring AS

explanation: MED suggests to the neighboring AS which entry point is preferred. Lower MED is preferred.

question: What is the OSPF hello interval on broadcast networks?
explanation: OSPF hello = 10s on broadcast/P2P, 30s on NBMA. Dead interval = 4x hello.

question: What is a route reflector in BGP?

options.text: A router that reflects iBGP routes to peers, eliminating full mesh requirement
A device reflecting traffic between VLANs

explanation: Route reflectors allow iBGP route sharing without full mesh, reducing session count.

question: How does RIPv2 differ from RIPv1?

options.text: RIPv2 supports VLSM, authentication, and multicast updates
RIPv1 is faster and supports more hops

explanation: RIPv2 added VLSM, MD5 auth, multicast (224.0.0.9), and next-hop. RIPv1 is classful.

question: What is the administrative distance of eBGP?
explanation: eBGP AD = 20 (very trusted). iBGP AD = 200. This makes eBGP routes preferred over most IGP routes.

question: What is the primary function of a stateful firewall?

options.text: It tracks connection state and allows return traffic for established sessions
It inspects all packets at layer 7
It blocks all incoming traffic by default

explanation: Stateful firewalls maintain a connection table, allowing return traffic for established connections.

question: Which IPsec mode encrypts both payload and original IP header?
explanation: Tunnel mode encrypts the entire original packet and adds a new IP header. Transport mode only encrypts the payload.

question: What is the difference between IDS and IPS?

options.text: IDS detects and alerts; IPS detects and blocks malicious traffic
IDS is Layer 2; IPS is Layer 7

explanation: IDS is passive (detect + alert). IPS is active (detect + block inline). Both analyze traffic patterns.

question: Which protocol establishes a secure VPN tunnel using digital certificates?
explanation: IPsec/IKE uses certificates or PSK for authentication. PPTP is old and insecure. GRE provides no encryption.

question: What is a DMZ in network security?

options.text: A segment for publicly accessible services between internal network and internet
A zone for employee personal devices

explanation: A DMZ hosts public services (web, email, DNS) with controlled access from both internet and internal network.

question: What does a WAF protect against?

options.text: Layer 7 attacks: SQL injection, XSS, CSRF targeting web applications

explanation: WAFs operate at Layer 7, inspecting HTTP/HTTPS for application-layer attacks.

question: What is 802.1X used for?

options.text: Port-based network access control authenticating users before granting access

explanation: 802.1X uses EAP with RADIUS to authenticate users/devices before allowing network port access.

question: What is PKI?

options.text: A framework for managing digital certificates and public-key encryption

explanation: PKI manages certificate creation, distribution, revocation for public-key crypto.

question: Which DDoS attack type consumes bandwidth by flooding the network?
explanation: Volumetric attacks consume bandwidth. Protocol attacks exhaust resources. Application attacks target Layer 7.

question: What differentiates SSL VPN from IPsec VPN?

options.text: SSL VPN uses a web browser (HTTPS); IPsec requires client software at Layer 3
IPsec only works with web traffic

explanation: SSL VPN works through firewalls via HTTPS with no client needed. IPsec operates at Layer 3 with dedicated client.

question: What is an ACL primarily used for?

options.text: Filtering traffic based on rules (source/dest IP, port, protocol)

explanation: ACLs define permit/deny rules based on packet attributes to control traffic flow.

question: What is a SYN flood attack?

options.text: Sending many SYN packets without completing the TCP handshake, exhausting server resources

explanation: SYN flood exploits TCP: attacker sends many SYNs but never ACKs, filling the half-open connection queue.

question: What is NAT's role in firewall contexts?

options.text: Hiding internal addresses and controlling address translation

explanation: Firewall NAT hides internal IPs, making it harder to target specific hosts.

question: Which protocol provides mutual authentication using certificates?
explanation: EAP-TLS uses digital certificates for mutual auth. PAP sends cleartext. RADIUS is a server protocol.

question: What is a honeypot?

options.text: A decoy system designed to attract and study attackers

explanation: Honeypots mimic real targets to attract attackers for study and early threat detection.

question: What does IKE Phase 1 accomplish?

options.text: Establishes the ISAKMP SA for negotiating IPsec parameters

explanation: IKE Phase 1 establishes the management channel. Phase 2 creates the IPsec SA for data encryption.

question: Which protocol provides AAA services?
explanation: RADIUS provides Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting.

question: What is MAC address filtering?

options.text: Allowing/denying network access based on device MAC addresses

explanation: MAC filtering allows only registered MACs. It provides weak security since MACs are easily spoofed.

question: What is a man-in-the-middle (MITM) attack?

options.text: Intercepting communication between two parties without their knowledge

explanation: MITM places attacker between parties to intercept/modify traffic. TLS and IPsec prevent MITM.

question: Which algorithm is commonly used in IPsec for bulk data encryption?
explanation: AES is the standard symmetric encryption for IPsec data. RSA is asymmetric (key exchange).

question: What is switch port security?

options.text: Limiting MAC addresses per port to prevent MAC flooding attacks

explanation: Port security limits learned MACs per port with actions when exceeded.

question: What is a zero-day vulnerability?

options.text: A previously unknown vulnerability with no available patch

explanation: Zero-days are unknown to vendors with no existing patch, making them especially dangerous.

question: What differentiates asymmetric from symmetric encryption?

options.text: Asymmetric uses public/private key pairs; symmetric uses a single shared key

explanation: Asymmetric (RSA, ECC) uses key pairs. Symmetric (AES, 3DES) uses one shared key.

question: What is network segmentation?

options.text: Dividing a network into isolated segments to improve security

explanation: Segmentation uses VLANs, subnets, or firewalls to create isolated zones.

question: What does the TLS handshake accomplish?

options.text: Authenticates server, negotiates encryption, establishes shared secret

explanation: TLS handshake: client hello, server hello + cert, key exchange, cipher spec, finished.

question: What is ARP spoofing (poisoning)?

options.text: Sending fake ARP messages to associate attacker's MAC with legitimate IP

explanation: ARP spoofing sends forged ARP replies linking attacker's MAC to a target IP.

question: Which firewall type inspects up to Layer 7?
explanation: Proxy firewalls understand specific protocols and inspect content at Layer 7.

question: What is a VACL used for?

options.text: Filtering traffic within the same VLAN

explanation: VACLs filter intra-VLAN traffic. RACLs filter inter-VLAN. PACLs filter on ports.

question: What is a DDoS amplification attack?

options.text: Using vulnerable third-party servers to amplify attack traffic toward target
Using compromised machines for legitimate traffic

explanation: Amplification exploits protocols (DNS, NTP, memcached) where small requests generate huge responses.

question: What distinguishes TACACS+ from RADIUS?

options.text: TACACS+ separates AAA and uses TCP; RADIUS combines auth/authz and uses UDP
TACACS+ uses UDP; RADIUS uses TCP

explanation: TACACS+ uses TCP, separates AAA, encrypts entire payload. RADIUS uses UDP, encrypts only passwords.

question: What is a SIEM system?

options.text: Centralized system collecting, correlating, and analyzing security logs and events

explanation: SIEM aggregates logs, correlates events, detects threats, and provides compliance reporting.

question: What does defense in depth mean?

options.text: Multiple layers of security controls across physical, network, host, and application layers

explanation: Defense in depth applies controls at multiple layers so if one fails, others still protect.

question: Which principle ensures users only have necessary access?
explanation: Least privilege grants minimum required access. Separation of duties splits critical tasks.

question: What is the difference between a virus and a worm?

options.text: A virus attaches to a host file; a worm self-replicates without a host
A worm needs a host; a virus spreads independently
A virus affects hardware; a worm affects software

explanation: Viruses need host programs. Worms are standalone and self-replicate across networks.

question: What is the purpose of IPsec SA (Security Association)?

options.text: To define the security parameters (encryption, authentication, keys) for a communication

explanation: IPsec SA is a unidirectional logical connection defining security parameters for protecting traffic.

question: Which Wi-Fi standard operates in both 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz bands with up to 6.93 Gbps?
explanation: 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) supports 2.4/5 GHz, OFDMA, 1024-QAM, and up to 9.6 Gbps theoretically.

question: What is the maximum theoretical speed of 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5)?
explanation: 802.11ac Wave 2 supports up to 6.93 Gbps with 4x4 MIMO, 160 MHz channels, and 256-QAM.

question: Which 802.11 standard was the first to use the 5 GHz band?
explanation: 802.11a was the first 5 GHz standard (54 Mbps). 802.11b used 2.4 GHz (11 Mbps).

question: What is MIMO in wireless networking?

options.text: Multiple-Input Multiple-Output using multiple antennas for increased throughput

explanation: MIMO uses multiple antennas to send/receive multiple data streams simultaneously, increasing throughput.

question: Which band does 802.11b/g/n use?
explanation: 802.11b/g/n support 2.4 GHz. 802.11a/ac use 5 GHz. 802.11n/ax support both bands.

question: What is the purpose of SSID hiding?

options.text: To prevent the network from appearing in passive scans (weak security measure)

explanation: SSID hiding removes the beacon but is easily discovered by active scanning. Not a real security measure.

question: Which wireless security protocol is considered most secure?
explanation: WPA3 uses SAE, 192-bit enterprise mode, and forward secrecy. WEP is broken. WPA has weaknesses.

question: What is a wireless access point (AP)?

options.text: A device that bridges wireless clients to a wired network
A router for wired connections only

explanation: An AP bridges wireless clients to the wired LAN, acting as a Layer 2 bridge.

question: What channel width does 802.11ac support for maximum throughput?
explanation: 802.11ac supports 20/40/80/160 MHz channels. Wider channels provide higher throughput but fewer available channels.

question: What is OFDMA in Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax)?

options.text: Dividing channels into sub-channels (RUs) for simultaneous transmission to multiple clients

explanation: OFDMA (Orthogonal Frequency-Division Multiple Access) divides a channel into smaller Resource Units for multi-user efficiency.

question: What is the main advantage of 5G over 4G LTE?

options.text: Higher throughput, lower latency, and massive device connectivity

explanation: 5G offers up to 20 Gbps, sub-1ms latency, 1M devices/km2, compared to 4G's 1 Gbps and ~10ms.

question: Which protocol is used for wireless client roaming between access points?
explanation: 802.11r reduces roaming time by accelerating the authentication process during BSS transitions.

question: What is the maximum data rate of 802.11b?
explanation: 802.11b supports up to 11 Mbps using DSSS/CCK in the 2.4 GHz band.

question: What does WPA2-Enterprise use for authentication?
explanation: WPA2-Enterprise uses 802.1X/EAP with a RADIUS server. WPA2-Personal uses a PSK.

question: What is co-channel interference in wireless networks?

options.text: Interference between devices using the same frequency channel
Interference between 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz bands
Signal degradation due to physical obstacles

explanation: Co-channel interference occurs when multiple APs or devices share the same channel in overlapping coverage areas.

question: Which wireless technology is designed for IoT devices with low power consumption?
explanation: 802.15.4 defines low-rate WPANs for IoT, used by Zigbee, Thread, and 6LoWPAN.

question: What is beamforming in wireless networks?

options.text: Directing wireless signals toward specific clients for improved signal strength

explanation: Beamforming uses multiple antennas to focus signals toward intended receivers, improving range and throughput.

question: How many non-overlapping channels are available in the 2.4 GHz band?
explanation: In 2.4 GHz, channels 1, 6, 11 (or 1, 5, 9, 13 in some regions) do not overlap. Each channel is 22 MHz wide.

question: What is TWT (Target Wake Time) in Wi-Fi 6?

options.text: Scheduling when devices wake to transmit, reducing power consumption

explanation: TWT allows APs to schedule device wake times, improving battery life for IoT devices.

question: What is the maximum number of spatial streams in 802.11ax?
explanation: 802.11ax supports up to 8 spatial streams (up from 4 in 802.11ac).

question: Which wireless authentication uses a pre-shared password for all users?
explanation: WPA2-PSK uses a single password for all users. Enterprise mode uses individual credentials via RADIUS.

question: What is the typical range of a Wi-Fi access point in an office environment?
explanation: Typical indoor range is 30-50m depending on obstacles, frequency, and antenna. Outdoor range can be much longer.

question: What is a wireless controller in enterprise WLAN architecture?

options.text: A centralized device managing multiple thin/ lightweight access points
A remote control for wireless devices
A DHCP server for wireless clients

explanation: Wireless controllers manage CAPWAP tunnels, configuration, and roaming across multiple thin APs.

question: What is BSS (Basic Service Set) in 802.11?

options.text: A single AP and its associated wireless clients
Multiple APs connected by a distribution system

explanation: BSS is one AP and its clients. ESS (Extended Service Set) connects multiple BSSs via a distribution system.

question: What is NB-IoT?

options.text: A narrowband cellular technology designed for low-power IoT devices

explanation: NB-IoT operates in licensed spectrum, providing low-power wide-area connectivity for IoT devices.

question: What is a VPC in cloud networking?

options.text: A virtual private cloud providing isolated virtual network within a cloud provider

explanation: A VPC is an isolated virtual network within AWS/Azure/GCP with private subnets, routing, and security groups.

question: What is SDN (Software-Defined Networking)?

options.text: Decoupling the control plane from the data plane for programmable network management
Replacing physical switches with virtual ones only

explanation: SDN separates control (decisions) from data (forwarding) planes, enabling centralized, programmable network management.

question: What is NFV (Network Functions Virtualization)?

options.text: Virtualizing network functions (firewall, load balancer) as software on commodity hardware

explanation: NFV replaces dedicated hardware appliances with software-based network functions on COTS servers.

question: Which AWS service provides DNS management?
explanation: Route 53 is AWS's DNS service. CloudFront is CDN. VPC is networking. ELB is load balancing.

question: What is the purpose of a cloud security group?

options.text: A virtual firewall controlling inbound and outbound traffic at the instance level

explanation: Security groups are stateful, instance-level virtual firewalls controlling traffic based on rules.

question: What is a VLAN in cloud networking equivalent to?
explanation: Cloud subnets within a VPC function similarly to VLANs, segmenting IP ranges and controlling broadcast domains.

question: What is hybrid cloud networking?

options.text: Connecting on-premises data centers with cloud environments using VPN or dedicated links

explanation: Hybrid cloud networking connects on-prem and cloud via VPN (site-to-site), Direct Connect, or ExpressRoute.

question: Which Azure service provides virtual networking?
explanation: Azure VNet provides isolated virtual networks with subnets, NSGs, and peering capabilities.

question: What is network peering in cloud environments?

options.text: Connecting two virtual networks (within or across regions) for private communication

explanation: VPC peering allows private connectivity between VPCs without traversing the public internet.

question: What is an overlay network in virtualized environments?

options.text: A virtual network built on top of a physical underlay network using encapsulation

explanation: Overlay networks (VXLAN, GRE) create virtual tunnels over physical networks for VM/container connectivity.

question: What is VXLAN used for?

options.text: Encapsulating Layer 2 frames in UDP for virtual network overlays with 24-bit VNIs

explanation: VXLAN supports 16 million segments (24-bit VNI) by encapsulating L2 in UDP (port 4789), overcoming VLAN's 4094 limit.

question: What is a NAT gateway in cloud networking?

options.text: A managed service providing outbound internet access for private subnet resources

explanation: Cloud NAT gateways allow resources in private subnets to access the internet while preventing inbound connections.

question: What is the difference between a security group and a network ACL in AWS?

options.text: Security groups are stateful and instance-level; NACLs are stateless and subnet-level
NACLs are stateful; security groups are stateless
Only one can be used at a time

explanation: Security groups: stateful, allow rules only, instance-level. NACLs: stateless, allow/deny, subnet-level, numbered rules.

question: What is SD-WAN's advantage in cloud connectivity?

options.text: Intelligent path selection across MPLS, internet, and cloud links with application awareness
Replacing all MPLS with internet only

explanation: SD-WAN optimizes cloud connectivity by selecting best paths based on application requirements and link quality.

question: What is the OpenFlow protocol used for?

options.text: Communicating between SDN controllers and switches

explanation: OpenFlow is the southbound API in SDN, allowing controllers to program switch forwarding tables.

question: What is a cloud load balancer?

options.text: Distributing incoming traffic across multiple backend instances for high availability

explanation: Cloud load balancers (ALB, NLB, ELB) distribute traffic across targets for availability and fault tolerance.

question: What is an Internet Gateway in AWS VPC?

options.text: A horizontally scaled gateway allowing communication between VPC and internet

explanation: Internet Gateways enable internet access for VPC resources with public IPs. NAT Gateways serve private subnet resources.

question: What is container networking?

options.text: Network connectivity between containers, often using virtual Ethernet pairs and overlay networks

explanation: Container networking (Docker, CNI) uses veth pairs, bridges, and overlay networks (VXLAN) for container connectivity.

question: What is the AWS Direct Connect service?

options.text: A dedicated network connection between on-premises and AWS bypassing the internet

explanation: Direct Connect provides dedicated, low-latency, private connectivity to AWS at speeds from 50 Mbps to 100 Gbps.

question: What is Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS)?

options.text: Cloud provider manages physical infrastructure; tenant manages OS, runtime, and applications
Tenant manages everything including physical servers

explanation: IaaS (AWS EC2, Azure VM) provides virtualized compute, storage, network. Tenant manages OS up. PaaS adds runtime. SaaS adds apps.

question: What is the difference between bandwidth and throughput?

options.text: Bandwidth is theoretical capacity; throughput is actual data transfer rate
Throughput is always higher than bandwidth
Bandwidth measures latency; throughput measures speed

explanation: Bandwidth is maximum theoretical capacity. Throughput is actual data transferred, always less than or equal to bandwidth.

question: What is latency in networking?

options.text: The time it takes for a packet to travel from source to destination
The total data transferred per second
The number of packets lost in transit
The number of hops a packet traverses

explanation: Latency is the round-trip or one-way delay, measured in milliseconds. It includes propagation, processing, and queuing delays.

question: What is jitter?

options.text: Variation in packet arrival times (delay variation)

explanation: Jitter is the variation in packet delay. High jitter degrades VoIP and video quality.

question: What is QoS (Quality of Service)?

options.text: Prioritizing certain types of traffic over others for predictable performance

explanation: QoS mechanisms (classification, marking, queuing) ensure critical traffic gets priority bandwidth and low latency.

question: Which QoS model provides guaranteed bandwidth and delay bounds?
explanation: IntServ uses RSVP for per-flow guarantees but scales poorly. DiffServ uses DSCP for class-based, scalable QoS.

question: What is traffic shaping?

options.text: Delaying packets to conform to a specified rate (bucket/token system)

explanation: Shaping delays packets to match a contracted rate (using token bucket). Policing drops or remarks excess.

question: What is the bandwidth-delay product (BDP)?

options.text: The amount of data that can be in transit on a link at any given time (BW x RTT)
The queue depth on a router

explanation: BDP = bandwidth (bps) x RTT (seconds). It determines the optimal TCP window size for full utilization.

question: What is a load balancer?

options.text: A device distributing incoming traffic across multiple servers

explanation: Load balancers distribute traffic across multiple backend servers for availability, scalability, and fault tolerance.

question: Which load balancing algorithm distributes based on server capacity?
explanation: Weighted round-robin assigns weights based on server capacity, sending more traffic to more powerful servers.

question: What is queuing delay?

options.text: The time a packet waits in a router's output queue before transmission
The time to process the packet header
The time for signal propagation on the wire

explanation: Queuing delay occurs when multiple packets contend for the same output interface. High congestion = high queuing delay.

question: What DSCP value is typically used for voice traffic?
explanation: EF (DSCP 46) is for VoIP with lowest latency. AF (Assured Forwarding) for business-critical. CS for signaling.

question: What is traffic policing?

options.text: Dropping or remarking packets that exceed a configured rate

explanation: Policing enforces a rate limit by dropping or remarking excess traffic. Shaping buffers excess packets.

question: What is TCP window scaling?

options.text: Extending the TCP receive window beyond 65,535 bytes for high-bandwidth links

explanation: Window scaling (RFC 7323) allows TCP window > 65,535 bytes, essential for high-BDP links.

question: What is packet loss?

options.text: Packets failing to reach their destination due to congestion or errors

explanation: Packet loss occurs due to congestion, errors, or buffer overflow. TCP retransmits lost packets; UDP does not.

question: What is Round-Trip Time (RTT)?

options.text: The total time for a packet to travel to destination and back

explanation: RTT = time from sending a packet to receiving its acknowledgment. Includes all delay components.

question: What is DiffServ (Differentiated Services)?

options.text: A QoS model that classifies and marks traffic for class-based treatment

explanation: DiffServ uses DSCP marking (6 bits, 64 values) to classify traffic into classes for differentiated treatment.

question: What is the optimal TCP window size for a link?

options.text: Equal to or greater than the bandwidth-delay product

explanation: Optimal TCP window >= BDP ensures the sender keeps the pipe full. Below BDP, bandwidth is underutilized.

question: What is a CDN (Content Delivery Network)?

options.text: A distributed network of edge servers caching content for faster delivery to users

explanation: CDNs cache content at edge locations near users, reducing latency and origin server load.

question: What is WFQ (Weighted Fair Queuing)?

options.text: A queuing algorithm that provides fair bandwidth allocation among flows based on weights
A priority queue for voice only

explanation: WFQ allocates bandwidth proportionally to flow weights, ensuring no single flow monopolizes the link.

question: What is the main cause of network congestion?
explanation: Congestion occurs when aggregate traffic demand exceeds link capacity, causing queues to fill and packets to drop.

question: What metric measures the percentage of sent packets that successfully arrive?
explanation: Packet delivery ratio = (received / sent) x 100%. Directly related to the loss rate.

question: What is an SLA in networking?

options.text: A contract defining guaranteed network performance levels (uptime, latency, bandwidth)

explanation: SLAs define measurable service commitments: uptime (99.9%), latency (<10ms), throughput (1 Gbps), etc.

question: What is header overhead in networking?

options.text: Non-payload bytes (headers) that consume bandwidth without carrying application data

explanation: Headers (Ethernet 14B + IP 20B + TCP 20B = 54B) reduce effective throughput. MTU 1500B means only 1446B is payload.

question: What is TCP slow start?

options.text: Starting with a small congestion window and doubling it each RTT until loss occurs
Waiting for ACK before sending each packet

explanation: TCP slow start begins with cwnd = 1 MSS, doubling each RTT to quickly probe available bandwidth.

question: What is network baseline?

options.text: A reference measurement of normal network performance for comparison during troubleshooting

explanation: Baselines record normal metrics (throughput, latency, loss) to compare against when issues occur.

question: What is SNMP used for?

options.text: Monitoring and managing network devices via a standardized protocol

explanation: SNMP (UDP 161/162) collects metrics, manages configurations, and sends traps/alerts from network devices.

question: What are the three versions of SNMP?
explanation: SNMPv1 (community strings, cleartext), v2c (bulk operations), v3 (USM auth/privacy, view-based ACL).

question: What is NetFlow?

options.text: A Cisco protocol for collecting IP traffic flow information for monitoring and analysis

explanation: NetFlow exports flow records (src/dst IP, ports, protocol, bytes, packets) to collectors for traffic analysis.

question: What is the SNMP manager also called?
explanation: The NMS queries agents and receives traps. Agents run on managed devices. MIBs define managed objects.

question: What is a MIB (Management Information Base)?

options.text: A hierarchical database of managed objects defining device parameters

explanation: MIBs define the structure of management data using OIDs. SNMP agents expose MIB data to managers.

question: What is sFlow?

options.text: A sampling-based traffic monitoring technology that captures packet headers at intervals

explanation: sFlow samples 1 in N packets and exports headers, providing scalable visibility with minimal overhead.

question: What is a Syslog server used for?

options.text: Centralized collection and storage of log messages from network devices

explanation: Syslog (UDP 514) collects system logs centrally for monitoring, troubleshooting, and compliance.

question: What is an SNMP trap?

options.text: An unsolicited notification from an agent to the manager about an event

explanation: Traps are asynchronous alerts sent by agents when events occur (link down, authentication failure, etc.).

question: What is NTP (Network Time Protocol)?

options.text: A protocol for synchronizing clocks across network devices

explanation: NTP (UDP 123) synchronizes device clocks to within milliseconds, essential for logging, authentication, and certificates.

question: What is network configuration management?

options.text: Tracking and managing device configurations, versions, and changes

explanation: Config management tracks changes, enables rollback, ensures consistency, and automates deployments.

question: What is FCAPS in network management?

options.text: Fault, Configuration, Accounting, Performance, Security management

explanation: FCAPS is the ISO network management model covering all aspects of network operations.

question: What is a network topology map?

options.text: A visual representation of network devices and their connections

explanation: Topology maps show physical and logical network layout, aiding troubleshooting and documentation.

question: What is the difference between NetFlow and sFlow?

options.text: NetFlow tracks every flow; sFlow samples packets statistically
sFlow tracks every flow; NetFlow samples
NetFlow is Layer 2; sFlow is Layer 3

explanation: NetFlow tracks complete flows (more accurate). sFlow samples packets (more scalable for high-speed links).

question: What is SNMPv3 USM?

options.text: User-based Security Model providing authentication and encryption

explanation: USM provides authentication (MD5/SHA) and privacy (DES/AES), addressing v1/v2c security weaknesses.

question: What is LLDP (Link Layer Discovery Protocol)?

options.text: A protocol for devices to advertise their identity and capabilities on a LAN

explanation: LLDP (802.1AB) enables neighbor discovery by advertising device info (chassis ID, port ID, capabilities).

question: What is the purpose of network monitoring?

options.text: Continuously tracking network health, performance, and availability

explanation: Monitoring tracks uptime, bandwidth, latency, errors, and resource utilization for proactive management.

question: What is an SNMP community string?

options.text: A plaintext password used in SNMPv1/v2c for authentication

explanation: Community strings ('public' for read, 'private' for write) are sent in cleartext, making SNMPv1/v2c insecure.

question: What is configuration backup important for?

options.text: Enabling quick recovery from device failures or misconfigurations

explanation: Config backups enable rollback to known-good states, critical for disaster recovery and change management.

question: What is RMON (Remote Monitoring)?

options.text: SNMP MIB extensions for proactive monitoring beyond basic counters

explanation: RMON provides advanced monitoring: history, alarm, event, and capture groups beyond standard SNMP MIB-II.

question: What is the primary purpose of a network management protocol?

options.text: To collect operational data and manage devices remotely

explanation: NMS protocols (SNMP, NetFlow, Syslog) collect data, manage configs, and send alerts for operational awareness.

question: What is the difference between SNMP GET and GET-NEXT?

options.text: GET retrieves a specific OID; GET-NEXT retrieves the next OID in the MIB tree
GET retrieves multiple OIDs; GET-NEXT retrieves one

explanation: GET fetches one specific OID. GET-NEXT walks the MIB tree, useful for table traversal in SNMP WALK.

question: What is an OID in SNMP?

options.text: Object Identifier - a unique numeric path identifying a managed object in the MIB tree

explanation: OIDs are dot-separated numbers (e.g., 1.3.6.1.2.1.1.1) uniquely identifying MIB objects hierarchically.

question: What is proactive network management?

options.text: Identifying and resolving issues before they impact users

explanation: Proactive management uses monitoring, baselines, and thresholds to detect and fix issues before outages.

question: What tool captures and analyzes network traffic packets?
explanation: Packet analyzers capture raw frames/packets for deep protocol analysis and troubleshooting.

question: What is a network baseline used for?

options.text: Comparing current performance against known-normal levels to detect anomalies

explanation: Baselines establish normal behavior patterns. Deviations from baseline indicate potential problems.

question: What is SNMP inform message?

options.text: A trap that requires acknowledgment from the manager

explanation: Informs are acknowledged traps (SNMPv2c/v3), ensuring delivery. Regular traps are unacknowledged.

question: How many bits are in an IPv6 address?
explanation: IPv6 addresses are 128 bits, written as 8 groups of 4 hex digits (e.g., 2001:0db8::1).

question: What is the IPv6 loopback address?
explanation: IPv6 loopback is ::1 (equivalent to 127.0.0.1 in IPv4). ::/128 is unspecified.

question: What is dual-stack deployment?

options.text: Running both IPv4 and IPv6 simultaneously on the same device/interface

explanation: Dual-stack runs both protocols natively. Each interface has both IPv4 and IPv6 addresses.

question: What is NAT64 used for?

options.text: Translating IPv6 clients to communicate with IPv4 servers

explanation: NAT64 allows IPv6-only clients to reach IPv4 servers by translating addresses and protocols.

question: What is the IPv6 link-local address range?
explanation: fe80::/10 is link-local (auto-configured, non-routable). fc00::/7 is ULA. 2001:db8::/32 is documentation.

question: What is the equivalent of IPv4 broadcast in IPv6?

options.text: IPv6 has no broadcast; it uses multicast and anycast instead

explanation: IPv6 eliminated broadcast. Multicast (ff00::/8) serves group communication. Anycast sends to nearest.

question: What is SLAAC in IPv6?

options.text: Stateless Address Autoconfiguration using EUI-64 or random interface IDs

explanation: SLAAC uses router advertisements to auto-configure addresses. Hosts generate interface IDs (EUI-64 or random).

question: What is 6to4 tunneling?

options.text: Encapsulating IPv6 packets in IPv4 for communication across IPv4 networks

explanation: 6to4 uses the 2002::/16 prefix with embedded IPv4 address for automatic IPv6-in-IPv4 tunneling.

question: What is the global unicast address range assigned by IANA?
explanation: 2000::/3 is global unicast (public routable). fc00::/7 is ULA (private). fe80::/10 is link-local.

question: What is Teredo tunneling?

options.text: IPv6 over UDP/IPv4 through NAT devices

explanation: Teredo encapsulates IPv6 in UDP (port 3544) over IPv4, working through NAT where 6to4 cannot.

question: Which IPv6 routing protocol is the equivalent of OSPFv2?
explanation: OSPFv3 extends OSPF for IPv6. It runs over IPv6 link-local addresses and separates protocol from addressing.

question: What is DHCPv6?

options.text: Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol for IPv6 address assignment

explanation: DHCPv6 provides stateful address assignment and configuration options (DNS, NTP) to IPv6 clients.

question: What is an IPv6 anycast address?

options.text: An address assigned to multiple interfaces; packets routed to nearest one
An address for all interfaces globally

explanation: Anycast addresses are shared by multiple devices. Packets are routed to the topologically nearest instance.

question: What is the IPv6 default hop limit?
explanation: IPv6 default hop limit is 64 (vs IPv4 TTL default of 128 on Windows, 64 on Linux).

question: What is the main advantage of IPv6 over IPv4?

options.text: Much larger address space (2^128 vs 2^32) and simplified header

explanation: IPv6 provides 340 undecillion addresses, simplified 40-byte header, no fragmentation, and built-in security (IPsec).

question: What is the maximum length of a Category 6 Ethernet cable run?
explanation: Cat6 maximum horizontal run is 100m (90m permanent + 10m patch), same as all UTP Ethernet standards.

question: Which cable type is immune to electromagnetic interference (EMI)?
explanation: Fiber optic uses light, making it completely immune to EMI. Copper cables (UTP, STP, coax) are susceptible.

question: What is PoE (Power over Ethernet)?

options.text: Delivering electrical power along with data over Ethernet cables

explanation: PoE (802.3af/at/bt) delivers DC power over Cat5e+ cables, powering APs, IP phones, cameras (up to 90W for 802.3bt).

question: What is single-mode fiber best suited for?
explanation: Single-mode fiber (9 micron core) has low loss over long distances. Multi-mode (50/62.5 micron) is for shorter runs (<500m).

question: Which Ethernet standard supports 25 Gbps over a single lane?
explanation: 25GBASE-CR (copper) / SR (multi-mode fiber) provides 25 Gbps per lane, used in data center leaf-spine architectures.

question: What is the T568B wiring standard pin order for RJ-45?

options.text: White-Orange, Orange, White-Green, Blue, White-Blue, Green, White-Brown, Brown
White-Green, Green, White-Orange, Blue, White-Blue, Orange, White-Brown, Brown
Blue, White-Blue, Green, White-Green, Orange, White-Orange, Brown, White-Brown

explanation: T568B: W-O, O, W-G, Bl, W-Bl, G, W-Br, Br. T568A swaps Orange and Green pairs. Crossover = one end T568A, one T568B.

question: What is the difference between multi-mode and single-mode fiber?

options.text: Multi-mode has larger core (50/62.5um) for shorter distances; single-mode has small core (9um) for long distance

explanation: Multi-mode allows multiple light paths but has modal dispersion limiting distance. Single-mode allows one path for long distance.

question: What does structured cabling refer to?

options.text: Standardized cabling architecture using hierarchical design with work area, horizontal, and backbone subsystems

explanation: Structured cabling (TIA-568/TIA-569) standardizes cabling into subsystems: entrance, equipment, backbone, horizontal, work area.

question: What is the maximum data rate supported by Category 6a cable?
explanation: Cat6a supports 10GBASE-T up to 100m with improved alien crosstalk performance. Regular Cat6 supports 10GBASE-T only to 55m.

question: What PoE standard delivers up to 90W of power?
explanation: 802.3bt Type 4 delivers up to 90W (71.3W to device). Type 3 = 60W. Type 2 (at) = 30W. Type 1 (af) = 15.4W.

question: Which Ethernet frame type is used for carrying VLAN-tagged traffic between switches?
explanation: 802.1Q inserts a 4-byte tag (TPID + TCI) into the Ethernet frame, enabling VLAN identification on trunk links.
